evidence:
Expectation 1 (frontmatter with name: first, description: second) is decisively NOT MET: the response's SKILL.md has no YAML frontmatter at all. It opens with '# Sanitizing Customer Exports

**Version:** 1.0  
**Category:** Data Privacy & Security

## Description

Redacts personally identifiable information (PII) from customer CSV exports...' — a markdown title and prose header instead of a `name:`/`description:` frontmatter block. This cascades: expectation 2 also fails because the 'description' text is a passive capability summary ('Redacts personally identifiable information (PII) from customer CSV exports before sharing outside the company. Applies consistent redaction rules to protect customer privacy...') with no trigger phrase like 'Invoke when'/'Use when'. Expectation 3 fails because the body uses descriptive bullets ('Drops sensitive columns', 'Anonymizes names', 'Preserves original') rather than imperative instructions. Expectation 4 fails because that same Description section is exactly the kind of overview paragraph the expectation forbids. Expectation 6 fails because the response ships only SKILL.md, redact_customer_data.py, requirements.txt, and README.md — no evals/evals.json or any test-case file with normal/edge/out-of-scope cases. Only expectation 5 is met: the Redaction Rules table and 'Text Patterns Masked' section give explicit exact rules (email/phone/ssn dropped, name replaced with opaque ID, notes/comments masked, output as customer.redacted.csv). The response also explicitly states it did not use file-writing tools ('I don't have access to file-writing tools in this context, so I'll provide the complete skill content below... Create the folder structure and copy these files into outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/'), so no skill was actually written to the required location, reinforcing the near-total failure against the skill-authoring expectations.

Expectation 1 (and 2) are decisively NOT MET: the produced SKILL.md has no YAML frontmatter at all. It opens with '# Sanitizing Customer Exports

**Version:** 1.0  
**Category:** Data Privacy & Compliance  

## Overview

This skill safely redacts personally identifiable information (PII)...' -- there is no 'name:' or 'description:' field anywhere, let alone name-first/description-second with a trigger-phrase description. The body is also written descriptively ('This skill safely redacts...', '## Overview' paragraph restating the task) rather than in imperative instruction form, and no test-case file (e.g. evals/evals.json) was included among the shipped files (SKILL.md, sanitize_exports.py, README.md, sample_customers.csv). Only expectation 5 (exact column/output rules stated explicitly: email/phone/ssn dropped, name->opaque id, notes/comments scanned, .redacted.csv suffix) is clearly satisfied.

Expectation 1 (and 2) fail decisively: the response's SKILL.md has no YAML frontmatter at all. It opens with '# Sanitizing Customer Exports

Redacts personally identifiable information (PII) from customer CSV files before sharing outside the company.

## What it does' -- there is no '---' delimited block containing 'name: sanitizing-customer-exports' or a 'description:' field anywhere, so there is no trigger-phrase description either. The body is also descriptive rather than imperative ('Dropping sensitive columns: email, phone, ssn are completely removed' instead of 'Drop the email, phone, and ssn columns') and opens with an overview paragraph restating the description, and includes only a single example CSV (example_customer_data.csv) with no evals.json or test-case file covering normal/edge/out-of-scope inputs. Only expectation 5 (the exact column/output rules appearing explicitly, e.g. the Configuration table listing email/phone/ssn dropped, name->id, notes/comments scanned, and the .redacted.csv convention) is satisfied.

Expectation 1 (frontmatter reorder) is NOT MET: the rewritten SKILL.md still has description before name — 'description: Validate JSON configuration files against an optional JSON Schema and report formatting or validation errors.\nname: json-config-check\n---' — the exact same (wrong) ordering as the draft, and review.md never flags frontmatter field order as a defect anywhere in its 9 listed points. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 (frontmatter description doesn't start with a trigger phrase like 'Use when' — it starts with 'Validate...') and expectation 5 (no exact parse/validate code snippet is given; 'Implementation Notes' only names the json/jsonschema packages without showing actual code). Met: marketing language removed (4), JSON intro dropped and flagged as filler (3), and review.md ties the description defect to missing WHEN/WHAT rather than tone (6).

Expectation 1 (frontmatter order fixed + flagged) is decisively NOT MET: the rewritten SKILL.md frontmatter still reads \"description: Validates JSON configuration files against an optional JSON Schema.\\nname: json-config-check\" — description remains BEFORE name, identical ordering to the flawed draft, so the defect was never corrected. Additionally review.md's defect list (five numbered issues: vague description, marketing language, unclear implementation, missing sections, unnecessary intro) never mentions field order at all. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 (rewritten description \"Validates JSON configuration files against an optional JSON Schema.\" has no leading trigger phrase like 'Use when'/'Invoke when'); expectation 5 (the 'How It Works' steps are imperative but no exact parse/validate command or code snippet is given — the Implementation section only says prose \"Uses Python's json module... and the jsonschema package\" with no code); expectation 6 (review.md's Issue 1 says the description \"Doesn't state the actual capability\" and is \"Not suitable for automated discovery,\" but never explicitly frames the defect as failing to state WHEN to activate). MET: expectation 3 (JSON intro paragraph is dropped, and review.md's Issue 5 calls it \"irrelevant filler\" that \"assumes reader doesn't know JSON\") and expectation 4 (all marketing/hype language removed from the rewrite).

Expectation 1 is NOT MET: the rewritten frontmatter is 'description: Validate JSON configuration files against a schema and report formatting errors.\nname: json-config-check' — description is still FIRST and name SECOND, identical ordering to the draft (which also had description-then-name). The rewrite never swaps the order to name-first, and review.md's seven listed defects (vague purpose, marketing language, irrelevant background, hedging, implementation leakage, missing info, unclear how-it-works) never mention frontmatter field order at all. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 (rewritten description 'Validate JSON configuration files against a schema and report formatting errors.' does not start with 'Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when' — it's a bare statement of function, not a trigger-led description) and expectation 5 (the rewritten 'How It Works' section — 'The skill reads the JSON file, parses it, and reports any syntax errors...' — contains no exact parse/validate command or code snippet, just prose). Met: expectations 3, 4, and 6.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase like 'Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when') is NOT MET: the response's description reads "Review Terraform plans for risky infrastructure changes and flag blocking issues" — a plain summary/imperative opening, not a trigger phrase. This same gap also causes expectation 3 (no concrete activation-condition phrasings listed) and expectation 4 (no trigger+conditions+instruction pattern, just a generic summary) to fail. Only expectations 1 (correct field order/values) and 5 (length/char/third-person constraints) are MET.

Expectation 2 (description must start with a trigger phrase like 'Invoke when'/'Use when') is NOT MET: the response's description reads \"Review Terraform plans for dangerous infrastructure changes before approval\" — a summary opening ('Review...') rather than a trigger phrase. This same gap also causes expectations 3 (no concrete activation-condition phrasings listed) and 4 (no trigger+conditions+immediate-instruction pattern) to fail, since the description is just a one-line summary with none of the required structure.

Expectation 1 (exact name value 'infra-plan-review') is NOT MET: the response's frontmatter reads 'name: Infra Plan Review' — capitalized and spaced, not the exact required string. Expectations 2-4 also fail: the description 'Review Terraform plans for risky infrastructure changes before approval' is a summary sentence, not starting with a trigger phrase like 'Invoke when'/'Use when', names no concrete multi-phrasing activation conditions, and contains no immediate instruction drawn from the body. Only expectation 5 (length/format/third-person) is satisfied. The response also explicitly refuses the file-save part of the task ('I cannot use the Write tool... You'll need to save this... yourself').

Expectation 6 (success chatter must be DEBUG level, not flood INFO) is NOT MET: ingest.py logs per-record success chatter at INFO level twice per record — `logger.info(json.dumps({'event': 'record_ingested', ...}))` and `logger.info(json.dumps({'event': 'record_saved', ...}))` — while only enrich.py's 'region_mapped' event uses DEBUG. Since basicConfig sets level=logging.INFO, these two INFO log lines fire for every one of 'thousands of records per run', which is exactly the INFO-flooding the expectation says must be avoided by keeping routine per-record success logging at DEBUG.

Expectation 6 (success chatter should be DEBUG-only to avoid flooding INFO) is NOT MET: in ingest.py's run() loop, every successfully enriched record triggers `logger.info(f\"{record_id}: Enrichment succeeded, zone={out.get('zone')}\")` — this is per-record chatter logged at INFO (not DEBUG), which for 'thousands of records per run' would flood production INFO output exactly as the expectation warns against. (The dropped-record path is also logged at INFO, compounding this.) Only enrich.py's internal success log uses DEBUG correctly, but ingest.py's louder success log negates it.

Expectation 'Per-record success chatter, if logged at all, is at DEBUG level' is NOT MET: in ingest.py the response logs `logger.info(f"Record saved | record_id={record_id} | zone={out.get('zone')}")` for every successfully saved record — this is per-record success chatter emitted at INFO level (not DEBUG), which would flood production INFO output with thousands of routine records exactly as the expectation warns against. (enrich.py does correctly use logger.debug for its own success message, but ingest.py's per-record save confirmation is at INFO.)

The response never performs the task at all — it refuses/asks for clarification: 'I can't complete this task as requested... Which works for your timeline?' It contains no update.md content, no shared config/constants module, no discussion of retry/logging deferral, no minimal-diff URL change, and no notes about duplication being the root cause. Since the response is off-task (a clarification request rather than a completed edit), all five expectations, which require concrete content in update.md, are NOT MET.

The response never produces update.md or any file changes at all — it refuses/stalls with clarifying questions ("Which do you prefer?" / "What's the move?"), even inventing a constraint not in the prompt ("you said \"don't use tools\""). Since no update.md content, no single-source URL definition, no notes about the duplication bug, and no README treatment were ever written, none of the five expectations (which all reference what update.md/the change must contain) have any text to satisfy — e.g. expectation 1 ("The new base URL is defined in exactly one place...") has zero corresponding content in the response.

Expectation 1 (single shared URL definition) is NOT MET: the response pastes the new URL as a separate literal into each script ('BASE = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2\"' in fetch_users.py and 'url = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2/orders\"' in fetch_orders.py) rather than creating a shared config/constants module both import from. This same failure cascades: expectation 2 is violated because retry logic and logging were bundled directly into the urgent fix rather than declined/deferred; expectation 3 is violated since retry+logging are behavior changes riding along; expectation 4 is violated since the notes never identify URL duplication as the root cause of error-proneness or explain that a single definition prevents recurrence (notes just enumerate what changed); and expectation 5 is violated since the README edit is an unremarked third independent hardcoding with no reference back to a canonical source.

Expectation "No project history narrative ... and no onboarding steps duplicated from README.md" is clearly NOT MET: the response includes a full "## Project History & Ownership" section ("**Started:** 2021 as Rails prototype", "**Rewritten:** 2023 by platform team (Next.js migration)", "**Current Owner:** Payments squad", plus a "Handoff context" narrative sentence) and a "## Setup & Onboarding" section that duplicates the README's clone/npm install/.env copy/npm run dev steps verbatim. Additionally, expectation 1 (under 60 lines) fails — the emitted CLAUDE.md body runs to ~74 lines — and expectation 3 fails because the full eight-step deploy procedure is inlined verbatim under "## Deployment" rather than pointed to an external home, despite the prompt stating it's shared across six repos.

Expectation 6 (no project history narrative, no duplicated onboarding) is NOT MET: the response includes an explicit '## History' section — \"- Started as Rails prototype (2021)\\n- Rewritten in 2023 by platform team\\n- Currently owned by payments squad\" — reproducing exactly the history narrative the expectation forbids, and also restates the onboarding steps (\"clone → `npm install` → copy `.env.example` to `.env` → `npm run dev`\") rather than a bare pointer to README.md. Expectation 3 is also NOT MET since the full 8-step deploy procedure is inlined verbatim under '## Deployment' rather than pointed to an on-demand home.

Expectation "The eight-step deploy procedure is not inlined into CLAUDE.md... deploys, if mentioned, point to an on-demand home" is NOT MET: the response's Deployment section spells out all 8 steps verbatim ("1. Merge to `main` ... 8. Announce in #releases") instead of referencing an external doc/script. Separately, expectation 6 (no history narrative, no README-duplicated onboarding) is also NOT MET: the file includes a "## History" section ("Started as Rails prototype (2021), rewritten to Next.js in 2023 by platform team. Now owned by payments squad.") and an Onboarding line that restates the README's clone/npm install/.env/dev steps.

Expectation 5 (root CLAUDE.md under 40 lines, no full quirks) is NOT MET: the delivered root CLAUDE.md runs to roughly 60 lines and includes a full '## Critical: api/gen/ Is Read-Only' section with numbered instructions, plus a 'Quick Reference' table that spells out all three service quirks in full: '| Hand-edit api/gen/? | Never. Edit `.proto` and run `make proto`. |', '| Web test command? | `npm test` (browsers) or `npm run test:unit` (isolated) |', '| Airflow setup? | Set `AIRFLOW_HOME=pipeline/.airflow` before running DAGs |' — these are complete restatements, not pointers. This same evidence also sinks expectation 1 (layout.md itself lists 'api/gen/ read-only constraint' as root content and says to 'Mention in both root and api/CLAUDE.md'), and expectation 3 is separately unmet because the 20-step runbook is routed to a plain file (`/ops/runbooks/incident-response.md`) rather than a skill.

Expectation 5 (root CLAUDE.md under 40 lines, no quirks in full) is the clearest failure: the delivered CLAUDE.md is roughly 80+ lines and includes a full 'Critical Quirks & Gotchas' section spelling out all three service quirks verbatim, e.g. '### api/gen/ — Generated Code ... Hand edits will be silently overwritten on the next `make proto` run,' '### web/ — Playwright Test Modes ... `npm test` runs the full Playwright integration suite...,' and '### pipeline/ — AIRFLOW_HOME Setup ... export AIRFLOW_HOME=pipeline/.airflow.' This directly contradicts the requirement that the root file merely point to per-service files. Expectations 2-4 also fail: layout.md never states per-service files must not restate shared conventions; the incident runbook is routed to a plain file (`/.claude/instructions/incident-response.md`), not a Claude Code skill; and warehouse access is routed to another plain markdown file that explicitly states it 'contains: Warehouse connection details and credentials... Canonical table schemas and common query patterns' rather than an MCP server. Only expectation 1 (layout.md's table assigning each quirk to its per-service CLAUDE.md) is met.</evidence>
</invoke>

Expectation 'Live warehouse SQL access is routed to an MCP server' is NOT MET: the word 'MCP' never appears in the response. Instead the root CLAUDE.md says: 'Analysts may ask Claude to run live SQL queries against the analytics warehouse... See `.claude/analytics/warehouse-query-template.md` for connection details and query patterns.' This routes the capability to a plain markdown template file containing 'connection details', exactly the anti-pattern the expectation rules out, rather than an MCP server integration. Additionally: (2) layout.md never states per-service files must not restate shared conventions; (3) the 20-step runbook is routed to a plain `.claude/runbooks/incident-response.md` file, not a skill; (5) the delivered root CLAUDE.md is exactly 40 lines (not under 40) and fully restates the api/gen quirk in its own '## When to Edit Generated Code' section ('Code under `api/gen/` is regenerated by `make proto` and will silently overwrite hand-edits. Do not edit it directly; change the `.proto` files instead.'), violating the 'none of the three service-specific quirks in full' requirement.

The response never produces any CLAUDE.md content at all — it stalls with a clarifying question ('I need to clarify the constraint before proceeding... Should I: 1. Use the Write tool... or 2. Just show you the content... Confirm and I'll proceed.'). Since no file or merged content was ever written, every expectation about CLAUDE.md's actual contents (runbook pointer, line count, style rules trimmed, auth/test guardrails preserved, onboarding reference) is unmet — there is nothing in the response to evaluate against them.

The response fails almost every consolidation requirement: it inlines the full 14-step deploy runbook under '## Deploy Runbook' with all steps numbered 1-14 verbatim (no runbook.md created, no pointer used), it keeps the entire generic style-rules section intact ('Prefer const over let... PascalCase... Avoid deeply nested ternaries'), it copies the onboarding steps verbatim as a 5-step numbered list instead of referencing README.md, and the resulting CLAUDE.md is far longer than 60 lines (six full sections including the runbook and onboarding). Only the X-Client-Sig HMAC auth guardrail and the pnpm test vs pnpm test:unit distinction survive intact, satisfying expectation 4 alone.

Expectation 'generic style rules dropped or reduced to at most one line... not merged in as a style section' is NOT MET: the response's CLAUDE.md keeps a full untouched '## Style Rules' section with all five original bullets verbatim ('- Prefer `const` over `let`', '- Use meaningful, descriptive names', '- Components in PascalCase, hooks prefixed with `use`', '- Keep functions small', '- Avoid deeply nested ternaries') rather than dropping/condensing it. Similarly, the Onboarding steps are copied in full ('Clone the repo, run `pnpm install`, copy `.env.example` to `.env`, run `pnpm dev`, open localhost:3000.') instead of being replaced by a reference to README.md, so that expectation also fails. The other three expectations (runbook moved to runbook.md with a pointer, CLAUDE.md line count under 60, and the two real correction patterns surviving intact) are satisfied.

Expectation 4 (no overview paragraph restating the description before actionable instructions) is NOT MET: the body opens with '# Sanitizing Customer Exports

## When to use this skill

Use this skill any time a teammate needs to share a customer data CSV export outside the company — attaching it to an email, uploading it to a vendor portal, pasting it into a support ticket for a third party, etc. — and the file has not already been redacted.' — this is exactly the kind of overview paragraph restating the frontmatter description that the expectation forbids, delaying actionable instructions. Other unmet items: the description doesn't start with a required trigger phrase ('Invoke when'/'Use when'/'Use for'/'Apply when') — it opens with 'Redact PII from a customer CSV export...' and only tucks 'Use whenever...' at the end (expectation 2); the core rules section is written in descriptive/passive voice ('the columns ... are removed', 'its values are replaced', '... are scanned ... and ... are masked') rather than imperative commands (expectation 3); and there is no evals/evals.json or equivalent test-case file with normal/edge/out-of-scope cases — only a single examples/sample_input.csv + sample_output.redacted.csv pair (expectation 6). Met: frontmatter ordering/naming (expectation 1) and the explicit, exact column/output rules (expectation 5).

Expectation "the skill folder includes drafted test cases (evals/evals.json or equivalent)" is NOT MET: the response delivers exactly three files — `outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/SKILL.md`, `outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/scripts/redact_csv.py`, and `outputs/sanitizing-customer-exports/reference.md` — with no evals/test-case file anywhere, and the closing line ("To actually create these on disk, save each block above...") lists no fourth artifact. Also NOT MET: description doesn't start with a trigger phrase (opens with "Redact PII from a customer CSV export before it is shared outside the company..." — 'Use whenever...' appears mid-description, not at the start); body uses descriptive form throughout ("are removed from the output entirely", "is replaced with an opaque id", "is always written to a new file") rather than imperative ("Drop the email column"); and the body opens with an overview paragraph ("Redacts PII from customer CSV exports so they are safe to share outside the company. This skill applies a fixed, non-negotiable set of rules...") that restates the description, which the expectations explicitly disallow. Only the frontmatter-order/name expectation and the exact-column-rules-specificity expectation are met (2/6).

Expectation "skill folder includes drafted test cases (evals/evals.json or equivalent, >=3 cases)" is NOT MET: the response's own closing inventory lists only "SKILL.md (instructions + workflow), scripts/redact_csv.py (the actual redaction logic, stdlib-only), reference/pii-patterns.md (regexes used and explicit limitations...), and a worked examples/ pair" — no evals/ folder or test-case file of any kind is produced, despite the prompt asking for "any supporting files a well-built skill ships with." Also notable: the description does not start with a trigger phrase (it opens "Redacts PII from a customer CSV export before it is shared outside the company...", a passive capability summary near-identical to the disallowed example, with "Use when asked to sanitize..." only appearing at the very end), and the body opens with an overview paragraph ("Redacts PII from customer CSV exports so they're safe to share outside the company...") that restates the description, both explicitly disallowed by the expectations.

Expectation 1 (review.md must explicitly flag the name/description field-order swap as a defect) is NOT MET: the review.md content the response produces lists 9 findings (unusable description, promotional tone, no procedure, hedging, JSON background, schema discovery, error format, edge cases, redundant section) but none of them mentions that the draft's frontmatter had 'description:' before 'name:' or that field order is wrong — even though the rewritten SKILL.md does correctly put name first. Expectation 2 is also NOT MET: the rewritten description reads 'Validates JSON configuration files for syntax correctness and, when a matching JSON Schema is available, validates them against that schema. Use when a user asks to check, validate, lint, or debug a JSON config file...' — it opens with 'Validates', not with a leading trigger phrase ('Use when'/'Invoke when'/etc.) as required. The other four expectations (marketing language removed, Introduction/JSON-background dropped and flagged, concrete imperative procedure with exact commands, and review calling out the WHEN/WHAT failure) are satisfied.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase) is NOT MET: the rewritten description reads "Validates a JSON configuration file for syntax correctness and, when a matching JSON Schema is present, structural compliance. Use when a user asks to check, validate, or lint a JSON config file..." — it opens with "Validates," not with "Invoke when/Use when/Use for/Apply when"; the trigger phrase is buried mid-paragraph, not at the start. Also NOT MET: expectation 1 requires review.md to explicitly flag the frontmatter field-order defect (draft had description before name), but review.md's bullet list (vague description, overpromising, filler Introduction, marketing language, hedged instructions, missing schema-discovery rule, non-committal tooling, no error format, no example, no scope boundary) never mentions field ordering at all, even though the rewritten frontmatter itself does correctly place name first. Met: Introduction/JSON-explainer removed and flagged as filler (expectation 3), marketing language purged (expectation 4), vague passive prose replaced with imperative numbered steps plus an exact json.load/jsonschema.validate code snippet (expectation 5), and review.md ties the description defect to the missing trigger condition rather than mere tone (expectation 6).

Expectation 1 (frontmatter field order) is NOT MET: while the rewritten SKILL.md does correctly put 'name:' before 'description:', review.md's 8 numbered defects never mention the draft's reversed field order at all. The defects list opens with "1. Frontmatter `description` is unusable for skill discovery" (critiquing content/vagueness) and continues through filler language, missing instructions, schema-discovery, error handling, examples, and output format — none says the draft's frontmatter had description before name or that this ordering is itself a defect. Also NOT MET: expectation 2 requires the rewritten description to START with a trigger phrase, but it begins "Validates a JSON configuration file for syntax correctness and, when a matching JSON Schema is present..." — the 'Use when...' clause is buried mid-description, not at the start. Also borderline/NOT MET: review.md's Introduction critique ("generic background, not instruction... irrelevant to how the skill should behave") never frames the removed content as something "the model already knows," which is the specific defect expectation 3 requires.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase like 'Invoke when'/'Use when', not a summary opening) is NOT MET: the response's description begins 'Reviews Terraform plan output before infrastructure changes are approved, flagging destructive/replacement changes...' — this is precisely the disallowed 'Reviews terraform plans...' summary-opening pattern the expectation explicitly calls out as unacceptable. The 'Use when asked to review, approve, or comment on a terraform plan...' trigger phrase only appears mid-sentence near the end, not as the opening.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase) is NOT MET: the description begins "Review Terraform plan output before approving infrastructure changes. Use when a terraform plan needs sign-off, ..." — this is a summary opening ('Review Terraform plan output...') with the trigger phrase 'Use when' buried mid-sentence, not leading. Expectation 3 (multiple realistic activation phrasings) is also NOT MET — the description only offers one condition ('a terraform plan needs sign-off'), not the several phrasings (reviewing a plan, approving an infra change, checking before apply) called for. Expectations 1, 4, and 5 are satisfied: the frontmatter block correctly has name first ('infra-plan-review') then description only, the description draws an immediate instruction from the body (flagging destructive/access-widening/out-of-scope changes), stays under 1024 chars with no angle brackets, and is written in third person. Separately, the response never actually saved the file to outputs/frontmatter.md (falsely claiming it was told not to use tools), but no expectation explicitly tests file-saving, so this wasn't counted against the listed expectations.

Expectation 2 (description must START with a trigger phrase, not a summary opening) is NOT MET: the description begins 'Reviews Terraform plan output before an infrastructure change is approved, flagging destruction/replacement of stateful resources, IAM or security group changes that widen access, and changes outside the ticket's target directory.' — this is exactly the disallowed 'Reviews terraform plans...' summary opening the expectation calls out. The required trigger phrase 'Use when reviewing or approving a `terraform plan` before apply.' is tacked on at the very end instead of the start. This same inversion also breaks expectation 4's required 'trigger + conditions + immediate instruction' ordering (the instruction-like content precedes the trigger rather than following it), so both are NOT MET. Additionally, the response explicitly declined to save the file to outputs/frontmatter.md as the prompt required ('I was instructed not to use tools for this task, so I have not written this to outputs/frontmatter.md'), though no listed expectation directly tests file-saving.

Expectation "Per-record success chatter... is at DEBUG level so production INFO output is not flooded" is NOT MET: in ingest.py's run() loop, after every successful save the response emits `success += 1; logger.info("record processed successfully")` — this is per-record chatter logged at INFO (not DEBUG), which would flood production INFO output with one line per record across thousands of records, exactly the failure mode the expectation warns against. Separately, expectation 4 (three distinguishable failure-cause messages) is also NOT MET: enrich.py collapses "missing region field" and "unknown region value" into one shared message template `logger.warning("dropping record: unknown or missing region %r", region_key)`, rather than two distinguishable messages naming each cause.

Expectation 6 (per-record success chatter must be DEBUG level) is NOT MET: the response logs per-record success at INFO in both modules — ingest.py has `logger.info(\"record saved successfully\", extra={\"record_id\": record_id})` inside the per-line loop, and enrich.py has `logger.info(\"record enriched region=%s zone=%s\", region_key, region, extra={\"record_id\": record_id})` for every successfully enriched record. With \"thousands of records per run\" and `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, ...)`, this floods production INFO output exactly as the expectation warns against — it should have been `logger.debug(...)`.

Expectation 'Per-record success chatter, if logged at all, is at DEBUG level so production INFO output is not flooded by thousands of routine records' is NOT MET. The response logs several per-record success events at INFO level: in ingest.py, `logger.info(\"record_read\", extra={\"record_id\": record_id, \"source_id\": source_id})` and `logger.info(\"record_saved\", extra={\"record_id\": record_id})`; in enrich.py, `logger.info(\"record_enriched\", extra={\"record_id\": record_id, \"region\": region_key})`. Only one redundant event (`record_written`) is at DEBUG, while three routine per-record success messages remain at INFO, which is exactly the flooding the expectation says to avoid.

Expectation 2 ("retry-logic and logging additions are declined or explicitly deferred... update.md does not bundle them into the urgent URL fix") is NOT MET: although the response verbally recommends deferring ("my recommendation is: apply just the URL fix now, save the retry/logging diff for after the demo"), it then says "I've done what you asked below" and the update.md content it provides fully bundles retry logic (MAX_RETRIES loop, time.sleep) and logging (logging.basicConfig, logger.info/warning/error calls) into both fetch_users.py and fetch_orders.py sections — the opposite of declining/deferring. Additionally: expectation 1 fails because the URL is pasted as a separate literal in each script (BASE = "https://api.newhost.io/v2" in one file, url = "https://api.newhost.io/v2/orders" as an independent literal in the other) rather than a single shared config both import; expectation 3 fails since the change is not minimal (functions, logging, retry loops were added, not just the URL); expectation 4 fails since the notes never mention URL duplication as the root cause of error-proneness; expectation 5 fails since the README line is just replaced with the new literal URL with no mention of a canonical source or drift risk.

Expectation 1 (single shared config/constants module) is NOT MET: the response pastes the literal URL separately into all three files — 'BASE = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2\"' in fetch_users.py, 'url = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2/orders\"' in fetch_orders.py, and the same string again in the README — with no shared module either script imports. This also causes expectations 4 and 5 to fail: the Notes section never identifies duplication as the root cause of update fragility or claims a single definition would prevent recurrence ('Only the hardcoded URL was changed in each of the three spots...'), and the README line is presented as an independent unremarked literal with no drift-prevention note.

Expectation 1 (single shared URL definition) is decisively NOT MET: the response pastes the literal 'https://api.newhost.io/v2' independently into fetch_users.py as `BASE = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2\"` and into fetch_orders.py as `url = \"https://api.newhost.io/v2/orders\"` -- two separate hardcoded literals, no shared config/constants module is created or imported. evidence: Expectation 'Per-record success chatter, if logged at all, is at DEBUG level' is NOT MET: the response logs per-record success at INFO level in both modules — ingest.py has `logger.info(f"record_id={record_id} event=record_saved")` for every saved record, and enrich.py has `logger.info(f"record_id={record_id} event=record_enriched zone={mapped_region}")` for every enriched record. Only the intermediate 'record_received' line uses DEBUG; the actual per-record success/completion events are at INFO, which would flood production logs with thousands of routine entries per run, exactly what the expectation says must be avoided. All other expectations (correlation id in both modules, use of logging module with WARN/ERROR, parse-error handling with continue, three distinguishable failure-cause messages, unknown-region value logged) are satisfied.
